description: Wednesday 17 June 2026 | Governance and Administration Committee Hearings of Evidence: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Internal Affairs (excluding the appropriations that are the responsibility of the Minister for the Community and Voluntary Sector and the Minister for Ethnic Communities) Hon Brooke van Velden, Minister of Internal Affairs Department of Internal Affairs Hoani Lambert, Acting Secretary for Internal Affairs John Sneyd, Acting Deputy Secretary, Regulatory and Identity Services Rachel Esson, Acting Deputy Secretary, Partnerships and Commissions Amanda Moran, Deputy Secretary, Policy and Te Tiriti Katrina Casey, Acting Deputy Secretary, Local Government Fergus Broom, Acting General Manager, Policy Jayne Beggs, Policy Director Brett Warwick, Acting Chief Financial Officer Greg Archer, General Manager Engagement, Data and Innovation Adrian Jarvis, General Manager Services And Access Darrin Sykes, Deputy Secretary, Enterprise Services Murray Davey, Chief Digital Officer, General Manager Enterprise Digital Services 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Internal Affairs (excluding the appropriations that are the responsibility of the Minister for the Community and Voluntary Sector and the Minister for Ethnic Communities); 2026/27 Estimates for appropriations within Vote Cities, Environment, Regions, and Transport that are the responsibility of the Minister of Local Government Hon Simon Watts, Minister of Local Government Department of Internal Affairs Hoani Lambert, Acting Secretary for Internal Affairs Katrina Casey, Acting Deputy Secretary Local Government Brett Warwick, Acting Chief Financial Officer Richard Ward, General Manager Local Government Policy, Partnerships and Operations Lori Hand, Executive Director Water Services Hemi Smiler, Executive Director Simplifying Local Government
descriptionSummary: Hearings of Evidence. 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Internal Affairs (excluding the appropriations that are the responsibility of the Minister for the Community and Voluntary Sector and the Minister for Ethnic Communities). Hon Brooke van Velden, Minister of Internal Affairs

chunks.text: and the team that have come in. Welcome to- They're still coming. Yeah, they're still coming through the door. You're our first minister that we've got here in DNA this morning, so great to have you with us for an hour. And I know that you will likely have an introduction for us, a short introduction, and then I will open up for questions.

So, I'll hand over to you. Fabulous. Thank you, Madam Chair. Well, good morning, everybody. And thank you for having me here this morning, as well as all my officials. And I expect, given this is an operational department, we'll also be relying heavily on their individual expertise. As Minister of Internal Affairs, I oversee a department that supports New Zealanders throughout their lives.

I expect it to do so efficiently, with careful cost management and a clear focus on delivering its core responsibilities. The Internal Affairs portfolio is broad. It includes identity and life event services, our national collections through Archives New Zealand and the National Library, regulatory functions, and support for major inquiries. I am also responsible for appropriations within Vote Internal Affairs.

I'm also responsible for two Crown entities, Fire Emergency New Zealand and the Classifications Office. And from the 1st of July this year, one Crown entity called Safety Net Critical Communications This includes reviewing their performance, participating in the setting and monitoring of their strategic direction and targets, and making appointments to their boards.

I'm pleased to report a strong start to this year, with my ministerial portfolio progressing a number of key legislative priorities. We have passed the Online Casino Gambling Act alongside the associated regulations, as well as the Regulatory Systems Amendment Act. I also acknowledge the role of this committee in that work.

I'm sure there was a lot of work that went into it, and so I very much appreciate your diligence over those matters. Since becoming minister, I've been pleased to see faster, more efficient services being delivered. New Zealanders are now receiving their passports in around three working days, down from 27 days of waiting when I came into office, and receiving citizenship in about 50 working days, down from a wait time of 180 days.

These processing times reflect sustained improvement in performance. The department continues to deliver value through its stewardship of New Zealand's knowledge and history. I recently, this year, launched ANZAC Stories, a new digital learning platform designed to help young people across the country explore ANZAC histories. This platform was developed by Archives New Zealand and the National Library, using national collections to make these stories more accessible to future generations. I recently opened the Heritage

Campus on 25 March this year, and the new archives building, Te Rua, was delivered on time and under budget. I've continued to champion work to strengthen key systems and services across this portfolio. I've also taken further action on public lending rights, proposing amendments to regulations to improve that scheme.

These updates aim to reduce administrative burden for both authors and the National Library, and simplify access to payments for authors. The department has made good progress in a disciplined fiscal environment, improving performance and focusing on its core role, and I'm confident that will continue this year.

Thank you, Madam Chair, and I welcome- Thank you... questions from the committee. Thank you, Minister. And I have first question from Mike, then Lisa. Thank you, Minister. I just have a question on the online casino gambling, because there's obviously been a significant decrease in funding from over six million to two million, and see the bill's passed and about to go into the auction process.

I'm just wondering if there's sufficient funding to actually do that process justice. So, the online casino gambling legislation and the regulations put in place the scheme for the licensing of online gambling. The functions of that regulator will be funded through levies directly onto the licensed providers, rather than coming directly from core funding.

So, I don't expect there to be any difficulty with overseeing the licensing process, because that will be directly funded by the expressions of interest process as well as the ongoing license year on year. And will online casino operators that were operating with New Zealand prior to that coming in place, will they be excluded from the auction process?

That is not my understanding. There shouldn't be anything prohibiting somebody putting forward an expressions of interest. It is simply a question of whether or not they meet the thresholds for the license itself, whether they meet the standards that the department and the secretary expect of them.

But there is no barrier to somebody putting forward an expression of interest, other than the $19,000, I believe, that needs to be stumped up for an expression. But weren't they operating illegally in New Zealand prior to this act? And do they have the social license, I guess, to then put an application in?

Well, the difficulty here is we do actually want to have a competitive market, and it's not for me as a minister to say that any one particular person will be precluded, given that I don't actually know who's going to be putting themselves forward. It's possible that only people who put themselves forward have never acted in the New Zealand market prior to this point, but it's also possible that the only people that we have coming forward are people who have acted in this space, and there won't be any new players.

It is really a question for the secretary as to whether or not they believe that the certain license conditions have been met as part of that expressions of interest process and the licensing auction. But it's not for me as a minister to preclude and say any particular person is in or out of the system at this point in time. Chair.

Very quick supplement. Thank you, Minister, for your opening comments. In terms of, and you've just alluded to the last question in terms of the Secretary of Internal Affairs, but I just wanted to ask, is your expectation, or can you explain the suitability or assessment criteria of those licensees have in terms of the budget? Will there be any work in terms of that?

Yes, I can ask our official here to go into more detail on the licensing process in terms of what sort of standards may be required of any license as part of the auction. Mōrena. John Sneed, Acting Dep Sec of Regulatory and Identity. So, there's effectively a two-stage assessment process. The first is an expression of interest process.

There will be a high-level assessment of suitability, meeting some pretty high-level criteria around, for example, association with Organised crime, for example. And anyone who passes that stage will be through the competitive auction process. Following the competitive auction process, which they'll they'll be assessed against a range of criteria, which will include their harm minimization standards, their age verification standards, consumer protection standards, basic minimum technical standards to ensure security and

integrity of the information they hold on the platform. So coming out of the auction process, there's still actually quite a significant assessment process against those criteria before someone will be licensed. Thank you. And just quickly. Yeah. And so the question will be, is the enough to cover those processes in terms of starting up the system?

We're comfortable that the funding system that's been set up is going to be able to deliver sufficient resources to the department to oversee these. Thank you. And Andy Hazell, sir. Yeah, so just on the criteria. So once you've got through the first hurdle, you're not involved with crime or anything like that. But the criteria, and just to follow up with Mike's question as well. The assertion that people are operating illegally, my understanding is that, in fact, they can't have been operating illegally because there was no law saying you

couldn't operate. There is now a law which says that you can't operate without having a license, so they would be operating illegally if they were to operate without a license going forward. But at the moment, in the past, they have not been because there has not been a law prohibiting it. Correct? That's correct. Yeah. It was unregulated. Right. Okay. So the area of question I just wanted to test was, so once we got through that first hurdle, the competitive auction, so clearly there's a whole range of different criteria. It's not just simply who's going to give the most money.

One of the criteria we did put in there was the New Zealand connection. There's one you didn't mention. I just wanted to check that that was actually going to be- Yeah... because it's now a requirement in the law. So a New Zealand connection is at least they say whether they've got one or haven't. And also that that's required to be considered.

It doesn't mean it's a determinative factor, but it is required to be considered. Look, I need to double-check and come back to the committee on that specific requirement. Certainly, there is a requirement for, for example, to have a New Zealand registered address so that we have- Yeah... have the opportunity to communicate with them. In terms of that specific point, I'll get back. It was things like, do they have people based in New Zealand in terms of employment? Do they have sort of benefit to New Zealand in terms of

beyond 4%, perhaps those kind of issues. Right. Yeah. There's no requirement to have a long-standing connection to New Zealand. It's not a requirement, it's a consideration. Mm-hmm. But the department is required to have that cons- to give consideration to that. Doesn't mean they've got to have it, but it's just a factor. Understood. Along with all the other ones you mentioned. Yeah.

Yeah. I mean, the difficulty of weighing up long-standing connection versus being based offshore, the important thing here is that they have an ability to be contacted in an online world for the regulations. There's also another difficulty here of if we restricted it to physically based- That's not what I'm saying... New Zealand platforms- That's not what I'm saying... you'd significantly reduce the market.

Yeah. So I'm not actually sure what your question is really pointing to. No, my question was there was a range of criteria as well as simply the financial criteria. The range of criteria, which are obviously to be considered, that was just simply one of them. It's just that you hadn't mentioned it. And it doesn't mean it's determinative in any way, but it is just one of those criteria.

Thank you for that. Okay. Catch you all. Thank you, and we'll move to Melissa. Thank you very much, Madam Chair. Thank you for being here, Minister. The Civic Information Service appropriation is the largest in the vote with $246.2 million. Which basically, it's to fund the operations to produce identity products, I'm guessing, passports and citizenship and all of that stuff.

But you actually talked about passports taking only three working days and citizenship 50 working days. How does that actually compare to when you became minister? Oh, thank you for the question. Look, the department has gone through quite a lot of increase in volume. We are expecting increase within the passport system because of the 10-year passport coming into play, and that taking effect for all the renewals this year.

As well as citizenship with the changes from labor coming through this year as well with the increase to residency pathways, now allowing people up to five years to citizenship. So we're expecting an increase in both sides. So the department has known that this increase is likely to happen this year and next year. And so we have been prioritizing efficiency of service.

When I first came into office, however, it was quite shocking to me some of the statistics and the service efficiencies within particularly the citizenship pathway. And so, for the last six years, the citizenship office has not met its targets.

However, for the last seven months we have consecutively met targets of having 90% processed within 75 days. That had not happened for the previous six years. Okay. And I'm really thrilled that the department has met the message that we need to ensure that New Zealanders, when they're accessing government services, receive timely service, and that we have efficiency top of mind.

And they have responded very well to that. One of the things that I asked of the department to do was to start prioritizing older cases first because- In one respect, it's very easy to try and meet your KPI of having as many processed quickly as possible, but then leaving the hard ones in the too hard basket.

And I was very annoyed and upset for individuals whose files were coming across my desk at the time when I encountered some that were years old, that had been sitting there in the too hard basket. Doing both at the same time of increasing the processing, which I'm happy for one of our officials to talk to some of that in detail about how they have done that operationally, as well as prioritizing those old backlog cases, has really ramped us up to be able to be prepared for the surge in demand that will be happening across both sides

of the citizenship and passport office. But one area that has changed through this term of government has been cross-skilling with the teams within passports and citizenship, so that the teams can fluctuate between working on just the citizenships or just the passports, but now working on both to ensure that we actually have the right people at the right time, keeping both of the wait times low.

Supplementary to that. Just talking about how you have managed to meet the target, did you set a different target, or was it the same target all the time, and it's a method of how they operated that actually increased the processing, I guess, productivity? Or did you actually input AI? Did you- No... What is the change that has actually seen this outcome?

Yeah. There's been a range of changes, and I'm happy for Hoani to talk to them in more detail. We have used more AI. We have also done more, like I say, of actually trying to focus on clearing the oldest files first, which has done an effect of bringing us up to having shorter wait times.

But do we have an official that can talk to the specific operational functions? Yes. I'll just ask Greg to come up, if that's okay. Thank you. And whilst he's making his way to the table. Good morning, everyone. I'm Hoani Lambert. I'm acting for Paul James, who is the secretary for DIA, and I'm the acting secretary currently. So as the minister was saying, the targets and standards for passports and citizenship have not changed.

The challenge that we had was actually meeting those targets as a department. So, the reduction in passport processing times has been significant. So we were pushing up near 30 days three years ago. We've managed to get that down now to three days. Now, that's well below standard, and there's a few reasons for that. Much of it is beyond our control, interestingly, and that is because of the challenges we've had with the impacts of conflict in the Middle East and what that has done in relation to projected demand for passports.

It's much lower at the moment, and that's largely due to the increased surge that we were anticipating being delayed. We are anticipating that as things settle in the Middle East, and that there's a way forward for the Strait of Hormuz, and then you eventually get confidence building with New Zealanders to start considering travel again, that we will see that surge start to manifest in our processing numbers. The underlying driver for the surge, of course, is the expiry of 10-year passports, which began this year. We're expecting our

normal average of processing per month of around about 38,000, or 480,000 passports a year. We were anticipating that to increase to around about 800,000 passports a year, or around about 70,000 passports per month on average. We have seen an increase, so we've lifted up to 53,000 for May.

But we are expecting that once those surge numbers start to manifest, that we will see the numbers of passport applications coming through the system hit that 70,000 mark and that surge to continue for around about four years through to 2030, '31. Which means we've been anticipating a surge which hasn't quite manifested, which means we've probably got more staff at the moment than we had required. That has delivered a greater than anticipated processing. But also, we have made some technological changes and some improvements to our digital servicing.

And just whilst we're on passports, I'll get Greg to talk about those. Yeah. So we've been running what we've been calling the Identity Services Acceleration Program for- Can you pull the microphone in? Sure. Yeah, so we've been running the Identity Services Acceleration Program for over 12 months, and so we've been focusing on just improving people, processes, and systems. And we've been doing a lot of work retraining staff to just make sure they're really competent. We've been looking to streamline processes, make them

more efficient, and we've been looking to improve our systems. With citizenship, for example, in March 2025, we went live with a major system upgrade, where we've automated the assessment of the system end to end. We've got interfaces with Immigration New Zealand, so we can API status and presence information. We do a range of automated calculations.

And so we can actually assess entitlement upfront in an automated fashion and then present those results to our staff if there's any exceptions, so they've got a clear picture of what they need to do. And we've seen significant backlog reductions because of that, so we're definitely around 15% more efficient in processing since we've gone live with those changes.

And as I've said, we've streamlined processes, and so we've just had a really big focus on bringing that backlog down. And at the moment, we've got about 10,000 citizenship applications in the queue, and that has been as high as almost 40,000. So yeah, we're just a full court press in that space. We've done the same thing in the passport space.

We've been really focusing on, again, clear automation where it's possible And so adult renewals, we do a range of automated checking, and if they are simpler applications, we expect those to process straight through, so no human touch. That used to be sitting at about 28% of adult renewal applications with straight-through process.

That's now sitting at 53%. And again, we've also done some things that, better training of our people, better processes, aligning ourselves. We ran a lot of capacity tests to just see how quickly we could process, and process without error. And so we put those new processes into the system to make sure that our people are effective, our processes are effective, and of course, we've really tuned our systems.

We-- Sorry. Last one because- We've- Too many... been going a wee while. Okay, too many questions. In terms of the automation and the AI basically included in the processing, and that you're talking about 100% fully automated, where does the accuracy, I mean- Yeah... lie in terms of checking to make sure that the citizenship, when people are actually given citizenship and getting New Zealand passports, is actually accurate?

Yes. So there are still two human checks for all citizenship application. It's more of a rules-based assessment, and it pulls information directly from authoritative sources. So we actually pull information from Immigration New Zealand that confirms that they are residents in New Zealand, they have permanent residence, they've been here for five years.

So we do a lot of what we would call automated system checking. Even with the criminal records and all of those checks that you're actually doing already- We have real time- All of those are actually done. Yeah. Yeah, we have real-time checks with the police, and they provide that information, and of course, if there's no criminal convictions, these things come through as all of these checks have been complete, and they are clear.

Our people then can make a simple assessment on that and move it through for processing. However, if there are exceptions, then our people obviously follow up with the police, handle those with customers, and then make determinations that go through different processes for submission. Yeah. Okay, thank you. Yeah, and just to follow up on that, if I can give a little bit of assurance that I'm constantly receiving submissions that don't meet the criteria as well, mostly where it comes to people who have not clearly met the five years

or don't clearly meet good character requirements. The minister individually must be satisfied that they do or decline them, whereas for the majority of people, they don't have a criminal conviction. They haven't done anything wrong. They have been here for five years, and they do want to follow the law. But for the exceptions, that's where a flag in the system is raised.

Okay, thank you. Limona. Thank you. Thanks, Minister. I want to ask a question around FENZ and then the vote, 3.492 million over the next two years. Can you explain how the estimated increased fuel costs will, over time, impact on that budgeted vote? And also, what has the price-- how did you get to that budget?

How well the workings in terms of that allocation for FENZ over the next two years? Yeah. Well, thank you very much. Look, I think everybody is aware that we've been going through a bit of a crisis globally as well as here in New Zealand with fuel shock. One of the things that the government has been very clear on is that no matter what happened, whether we were at sort of one, two, three, or four, it was crucial that our fire and emergency system as well as all of our emergency responders- Mm-hmm... had access to fuel suppliers to ensure that we

still had capacity around the country. As part of the budget process, I was asked by the Minister of Finance to put in a budget bid for fire and emergency, and so for the particular numbers of how much was needed for fire and emergency, that came down to officials from the Department of Internal Affairs as well as Fire and Emergency had to come up with some specific numbers for how much extra they thought they might need for added capacity.

However, that was also part of a Treasury-based analysis rather than from my direct office. But I'm happy for Amanda here to speak more to that. Good morning. Amanda Moran. So in the budget, there was 2.278 million included for covering Fire and Emergency's additional potential fuel costs.

And that, as was set out in the way that the minister's described, including looking at general processes across all of government to make sure that there was an equitable distribution of the money available to help with the fuel crisis. Thank you. Yeah. And just in terms of prioritizing those response activities within the budget line with Treasury, can you elaborate that two years? Do you think that that will be enough based on those workings for FENZ in terms of that vote specific?

Look, at this particular juncture in time, I do feel confident in that. Clearly, we are dealing with a very dynamic global situation. I'm very happy to report even in this last week, it appears that, or at least I shouldn't get too ahead of myself. It appears that we have some form of consensus happening in the Middle East, where the strait will be reopened.

And it really does come down to what the fuel prices will be doing day to day, week to week. It is near impossible to forecast out- Sure... based on what will be happening globally. However, I do know that Fire and Emergency did their best to forecast out based on a range of different factors.

But how they came to those particular different factors is probably best directed at Fire and Emergency officials themselves as to their own assumptions that they built into how much did they want to request from Treasury. Thank you. Okay. Lisa. Just want to... Different topic.

Want to talk about, in your opening address, you actually talk about the collaboration between the National Archives and the Library Services on Anzac Stories. Have they ever collaborated? Because most people would actually think that archives are where you actually store old things, and library, basically, it's a library. So what is this Anzac Stories and what prompted that kind of collaboration?

Oh. And are there going to be any other such? Because often people don't think of archives and libraries. Yeah. Well, thank you for the question. Look, I'm really thrilled by the work that archives and libraries have done in this collaboration. They haven't worked on a digital collaboration before, which is really what I was asking for as a minister.

One of the reasons why was because I deeply believe in the proper education of our next generations coming through the schooling system, and one of the things that I'm very passionate about is ensuring that we know about our history here in New Zealand. And, I'm very thankful that for myself, my mother has made me very aware of our connection to the wars, and my great-grandfather fought at Gallipoli himself, and I've been very aware of that story in our family. But I know for some other members of our community, that's not the case, but they want to know how they

can be attached to this country and what our shared history is. When it came to delving into some of our national days, in particular for Anzac Day, I asked our library as well as our archive to work together, on the sources that they had digitized to make them accessible in a child-friendly way for the next generations. And what's come out has been a really good product. So Anzac Stories, which is featured on the library's website, delves into depths of individual stories that have been written by a children's book author, then checked with war historians to

make sure they are factually accurate, using footage as well as archival material from both library and archives to build those collaborative stories. Now, I know from some very early feedback that I've received, there's been 30,000 views of that particular page, which I understand is much higher than we usually get on the National Library website, for about eight minutes a session, which is also very unusually long, which means that people are actually engaging with the material. And I hope that over years to come, they will engage further.

But we do have our chief librarian here to talk further about that. Morena. Rachel Essen, national librarian. And just to add to the statistics that the minister's just shared, the other thing is that it's driving people who haven't previously used National Library and Archives to the site.

So approximately 54% of those 30,000 visits are from people who hadn't been to the site before. But it's been the opportunity, with the new heritage campus for archives and library to work together more closely. This is a natural progression of that, so we've physically come together.

We've got a new structure that brings us together organizationally, and now that means that we're able to do a lot more, and produce things like the Anzac Stories. This is intended as a pilot. We've learnt from how to deliver that, and we will be looking at other topics that we can deliver. And it was a really great co-design process. We involved two local schools, Belmont School and Adventure School in Porirua. And children from those schools actually had an opportunity to model the site and provide feedback and particularly suggest the

interactive aspects of it, so the quizzes and things. And they came to the launch, when the minister launched the site, and it was just great. We also took the opportunity to show them physical things from the collection. And other research has shown that once you've engaged with something online, you're more interested in seeing it physically. So it was great.

Looking forward to more. Awesome. You guys are ignored. Yeah. National Archives and the libraries. That's right. Wonderful work. Thank you. Andy, you want to jump on that? Yeah, just a quick sup because I actually asked this question yesterday when we had MCH in front of another committee. And, it was a question about Ngā Tapuwae and the various different interpretation that they do.

Mm-hmm. But so thinking of your relationship together, Archives and the National Library, is there any sort of cross-conversation? I know MCH is not talking about a children's approach, a children's specific education. It's more about general. But what level of collaboration is there or should there be? Is that an opportunity to do things better?

And I know I specifically asked that question yesterday because some of our historians are a little disturbed that we've kind of dropped the ball a little bit after the World War I commemorations. Yes. In that area. Not so much, not here, but in that area. Mm-hmm. We definitely collaborate really well with our colleagues from Culture and Heritage. And we're very aware of the work that they're doing.

We've also been in discussions with them to be able to archive any of their website or material that they're not going to keep updating if that's decisions that are made. So we work really closely with them to make sure that the overall picture is preserved and available and accessible for New Zealanders, wherever they might be.

So that sounds as if... Because the question which got raised, this was at a fairness book town event with Chris Pugsley- Mm-hmm... Carter. And the question which was raised, who is the best curator-... the best guardian for all of this kind of material? Because it did feel like MCH had kind of backed away a little bit Um, and that's why I'll leave that question with you two.

I'd be interested in your answers- Mm... to whether in fact it is the National Library or the archives- Mm... that are perhaps the better curator for that MCH. Well, we certainly operate as a system, as a heritage system. We collaborate really closely with our colleagues at Te Papa Tongarewa- Yeah... and MCH, and right across Archives New Zealand and the National Library both have system leadership roles, which we take really seriously, and so we, uh, continually engage and work to support each other and collaborate. Uh, I think there's always more that can be done in

that space. So the door is open in terms of that, Rachel. So did you want to talk about the role of the National Library in terms of preservation- Mm... of websites? Yeah. Um, so that is something that is part of our mandate. Yeah. So we collect and preserve information that's published, and that includes online information and includes websites.

Uh, so we do a whole of domain harvest once a year, a snapshot of everything. Uh, but we also have specific websites that we preserve and collect and make accessible. So if we asked, final question there, if we asked who the ultimate guardian of these, um, uh, national memories is, it really sounds as though it's the National Library more than anybody else.

Well, I think- So if you saw others who were not doing what you thought they would do, you would sort of- Absolutely... say, "Hey, you've got to do something about that," or alternatively- Yeah. And our collection policy does state that- Yeah... is that, if it's of national significance, uh, we're the repository for it. Um, the Turnbull Library collections are kept in perpetuity.

Uh, archives collections are kept according to their, um, uh, disposal authority and we take that responsibility very seriously. Mm. Do you think that's a common thread between Archives New Zealand, the National Library, and the Alexander Turnbull, is that they are holding the documentary heritage for New Zealand. And so making sure that these institutions are working in an integrated way together in partnership with other ministries like MCH is extremely important.

Yes, absolutely. And my vision for all of this, um, work, starting with ANZAC stories, is to actually make it more accessible to people because, you talk to an average man on the street, they can't tell you that they've ever engaged with the archives before. Um, National Library is not much better. But given that we have such a digital focus now of preserving our material and making sure it's online for all, how do we ensure that it's actually used by people?

Yeah. Right. Well, thank you. Limana. Thank you. Um, Minister, I'd just like to go back to, um, foreign emergency in terms of, um, Internal Affairs cuts, and I wanted to understand better, um, or have a breakdown from you in terms of cuts, um, to the, uh, Internal Affairs. And do you receive regular updates from the department in terms of the vote specific of those cuts?

Uh, is there anything in particular when you're referring to the cuts? I'm just conscious 95% of the funding is actually through levies rather than government funding. Get that, and we understand that the Crown contribution is maintained at $8 million. Mm-hmm. Um, but specifically, uh, a number of us or I specifically have visited fire stations at the request of those, and there is real concern around their fleet management.

Which, um, you know, that we're doing an inquiry on, but specifically around the reduction, um, of the vote in terms of foreign emergency. Look, when it comes to fire and emergency and the overall confidence that I have in their asset management and fleet procurement and property management, um, it's clear that fire and emergency is on a continuous path of improvement.

Um, and that's where my confidence in fire and emergency comes from. In past years, they have not had plans, um, whereas in this term of government, we at least have an asset management plan. Now, the difficulty here comes from how that plan is then going to be actioned. Um, the last meeting I had with the board, I was given an assurance that they are actually on track, uh, with their asset management plan.

However, uh, the difficulty is squaring that away, uh, with the information that then comes to me, uh, from some of my officials. And so, if I think of, uh, the fact that, um, we have a little bit of a discrepancy between what I'm being currently told of that we are actually meeting our asset management versus the fact that, um- Are not... I don't necessarily believe that they will actually deliver their capital program this year.

Um, I'm also advised that this year, uh, there is again going to be an underspend of about 24 million against a forecast of 117 million due to delays in property and fleet procurement. Um, and I'm aware that this is not an isolated occurrence. government. Welcome, Minister, and welcome to your team here. We've got you to 10:15, but I just want to let you know that I'm going to be leaving for another session halfway through or- Mm-hmm... a third of the way through, perhaps. And so Tim will take over at that stage.

Zealand with a significant influence on infrastructure, economic development, planning, and local communities. We have a significant work program underway in local government, starting with the delivery of water services and moving into the structure of local government. It's important that we continue that work to ensure that local government is fit for purpose, represents value for money, and is delivering the best results for, and outcomes for ratepayers.

As minister, this has been and remains a priority for me. Having reliable and financially sustainable water services is a key priority for this government. The Local Water Done Well programme has seen plans put in place for every council, providing a clear way forward for investment and delivery.

Overseeing delivery of these plans will be significant, and also ensuring public services are delivered efficiently is also a priority. New Zealanders are concerned about the increase in their council tax bills, and local councils must focus on delivering the core services and greater efficiencies to manage costs. This is why we're implementing a rates capping policy.

Doing so will help ensure councils live within their means and keep rates increases at a manageable level. Simplifying local government is also a key area of reform underway by this government. It will help reduce duplication of services, speed up decision-making, and help councils make better decisions and work together. Local councils have already signaled their keenness to progress this work, and we have provided a voluntary fast-track option for those who want to go faster in this area.

It's important that we do this in order to support the new planning system, which will also come with the Resource Management Act reform that is currently underway. The City Regional Deals programme is also a key area of reform that we have underway, supporting greater partnership between central and local government in areas that matter to unlock economic growth and potential.

The local government systems improvement legislation going through the House at the moment will also help reduce the regulatory burden on councils. Reviewing the dog control legislation will also be a focus for the coming year. New Zealanders are rightly concerned about dog attacks and the surging number of unregistered dogs. The current legislation is no longer fit for purpose.

Finally, a new Ministry for Cities, Environment, Regions and Transport will be established on the 1st of July 2026. This will bring together the ministries, the environment, housing, transport, together with the local government policy and operational functions from the Department of Internal Affairs. It will bring together key legislative and regulatory functions support effective local government, making central government more coordinated and streamlined in how it works with councils.

I'm pleased to see such a comprehensive and ambitious set of changes introduced by this government. Just got a series of hopefully quick questions around local government reform to start things off, particularly around the Head Start process that you've identified. Essentially, the government has given councils a three-month or 90-day period to engage with communities around whether or not they are going to avail themselves of the Head Start process.

Do you think that is going to be a sufficient period of time to ensure that there aren't any perhaps opportunities where councils are willing to engage but maybe just don't have the time in terms of capacity to be able to do that appropriately? Yes. Well, first and foremost, I'll refer you to the document guidance for Head Start, which includes a timeline table there in the pack, which actually outlines the full steps through to 2028, including the date in which you're referring to on the 9th of August, which is when we

expect councils who choose to do so to submit outline proposals at that point. That is the first milestone on that journey towards 2028 reforms. And again, as the foreword of this document outlines very clearly at the start that this is a voluntary process, and we have consulted with local government councils across the country, and their feedback was very clear. A number of them want to progress with reform, a number of them want to get on with that process, and the Head Start process provides them with that mechanism to do so.

If councils are not in the position to meet the requirements of the Head Start process, then that is their choice. This is a voluntary process and is not mandatory for any council to make a submission into that process. Have you heard yourself from councils who have expressed a desire to have a little bit longer than the three-month timeframe?

It'd be fair to say that, and as you know, we have 67 councils across the country. There is a range of views in regards to that. But the consultation which we did in a public manner over the Christmas period came back with clear feedback from a wide range of councils that they wanted a process that allowed them to lock in the first stage of their reform process before the 2028 local body elections to give them time to bed that change in, hence the Head Start process put in place, that allows a mechanism for those who

choose to submit a plan to do so. It'd be fair to say that there are some councils that are Are less advanced in regards to their planning around how they may look to simplify their functions. That is not a surprise. And for those councils, they should take their time in regards to planning what will work for their communities.

What we're interested in is the councils that do have a clear understanding of where they want to go and how they want to get there. We now have a mechanism to, in effect, take on board their plans and progress legislative reform in their area. Again, there's going to be differing groups depending on what they choose. The alternative, of course, is what you have described as the backstop.

Are you satisfied that there is sufficient information out there around what the backstop will actually look like for those that don't voluntarily engage in this process? Well, the government outlined when we released our guidance for the broader reform of local government, when we stipulated that we will be removing regional councils from the 2028 election period.

And the feedback from that consultation process was very clear. There were a number of councils that looked at the backstop proposal that we consulted upon and said, "Look, we believe there's changes and modifications to that model." There were others that said, "We want the ability to actually structure our own governance to meet our requirements within our local area," which was different to what we consulted on.

The feedback across the board by Cabinet was that we will continue to take feedback in regards to that backstop process and the final design of it, of which Cabinet has not made a decision on the final design at this point. However, we will look to do that in 2027. Right here, right now, we are focused on working with those councils that have a clear plan around what they want to do and working with them to support them to make an application under the Head Start pathway.

And between those two mechanisms, on the 9th of August, we'll know who has applied on the Head Start and who hasn't, and then we'll be able to work with all councils. The key objective here is to ensure that we move from where we are today to a more simplified local government model. And the feedback I've had, and it's positive to hear, is that the opposition parties are supportive of this direction of travel.

And the voluntary nature of which is important because it allows communities to move. But there are communities that are moving at different pace. It provides a voluntary, streamlined option for councils that want to lead transition early. And that is the mechanism for those to do so. At that point, we'll work through. Again, I go back to the timeline between now and 2028. The period that we have in order to undertake this reform is between now and 2028. The 9th of August is a milestone on that journey for some councils, but not all.

It gives all councils space and time to work through the detailed needs of their community

and their requirements. Regional councils will not-- councils will not exist beyond 2028. I'm confident that that period of time between now and 2028 elections provides adequate time to work through this process.

What's most important for government, and actually getting feedback from councils and mayors and ratepayers, and actually feedback that I've heard from aspects of the opposition is, is that any reform needs to be enduring, and it needs to avoid a mechanism where, in effect, there is a position of change of that fundamental policy.
